substack_posts.title: The System of Action: Why "Close the Books" Is Harder Than Any Model
substack_posts.summary: An operator speaks in business verbs. Six enterprise systems speak in incompatible APIs. Here's the layer in between — and the demo where the right answer was to stop.

substack_posts.title: Stop Tuning Prompts. Build the Feedback Loop.
substack_posts.summary: How RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards) turns static agent pipelines into systems that get better on every run.

substack_posts.title: Your LLM Is Smarter Than You Think. Your Architecture Is Holding It Back.
substack_posts.summary: The architecture nobody talks about behind LLMs that actually work in production

linkedin_posts.title: Loop engineering: at 95% success per step, a 20-step task finishes correctly only ~36% of the time. Every point you claw back lives in the loop.
The models have converged. Intelligence is becoming a utility — defensibility has moved one layer down, to coordination.
"AI agent" is the most overused word in enterprise tech right now — and the most misunderstood. The real control surface is the harness.

x_posts.title: Operators don't think in APIs. They think in verbs. Check the balance. Update the pricing. Close the books.
One business command goes in. Underneath, the coordination layer understands context, chooses the right tool, and applies policy + approval before it writes.
The demo that makes the point: "Close the books for last month, and schedule a close on the 1st." It came back blocked. On purpose.

pinned_work.title: Stop Tuning Prompts. Build the Feedback Loop.
